critic_raw:
The causal DAG is a free parameter that encodes assumptions about which variables are biasing and which relationships are admissible. DECAF's fairness guarantee is conditional on the DAG being correctly specified. A misspecified DAG — which is likely in real datasets where the true causal structure is unknown — can cause DECAF to remove the wrong dependencies or preserve biasing pathways it was supposed to eliminate. The replication study needs to evaluate sensitivity to DAG misspecification.

Fairness metrics (demographic parity, equalized odds, calibration) are mathematically in tension with each other when base rates differ across groups. DECAF optimizing for one fairness criterion may worsen another. A replication that evaluates only the metric the original paper targeted cannot demonstrate general fairness — and may even make other fairness criteria worse.

The downstream model evaluation uses models trained on DECAF-generated synthetic data and evaluated on held-out synthetic data from the same generation process. If the synthetic test set shares the GAN's distributional artifacts with the synthetic training set, the evaluation measures GAN consistency rather than real-world fairness. Evaluation against real held-out data (not synthetic) is the meaningful test.
all_issues_raised: DECAF's fairness guarantee is conditional on a correctly specified causal DAG; DAG misspecification — likely in real datasets with unknown causal structure — can preserve biasing pathways, and sensitivity to DAG specification is not evaluated.

Fairness metrics (demographic parity, equalized odds, calibration) are mutually in tension when base rates differ; optimizing for one may worsen others, and evaluating only the paper's target metric does not demonstrate general fairness.

Evaluating downstream models trained and tested on synthetic data from the same GAN measures distributional consistency of the GAN, not real-world fairness; evaluation on real held-out data is the meaningful test.
all_issues_adjudicated: DECAF's fairness guarantee is conditional on a correctly specified causal DAG; DAG misspecification — likely in real datasets with unknown causal structure — can preserve biasing pathways, and sensitivity to DAG specification is not evaluated.

Fairness metrics (demographic parity, equalized odds, calibration) are mutually in tension when base rates differ; optimizing for one may worsen others, and evaluating only the paper's target metric does not demonstrate general fairness.

Evaluating downstream models trained and tested on synthetic data from the same GAN measures distributional consistency of the GAN, not real-world fairness; evaluation on real held-out data is the meaningful test.
